summary: Stancil argues that most LLM analytics chatbots are misaligned with real analytical work because they answer requests without the context needed to produce useful or correct outputs. He proposes a “smol analyst” workflow—iterative, feedback-driven drafting like a junior analyst—which could make LLMs more practical, while noting that data’s need for transparent logic makes this harder than in software.
money_quotes: If LLMs do end up changing how we use computers, this type of approach—one that introduces new ways of accomplishing some task rather than accelerating old ways; the car rather than the faster horse—feels like a glimpse of the future.
If we have to ask a bunch of follow-up questions before we dig up some number for people, why are we excited about LLMs that mechanically do exactly that?
Instead of asking for answers, people describe the report or dashboard that they want to create.
In data, black boxes don’t work.
The only way to know for sure what a query or Python script does is by reading the query or Python script.
tone: analytical and skeptical with a reflective, lightly humorous edge
key_insight: LLM analytics will be most useful when treated like junior analysts in an iterative feedback loop, but their outputs still require transparency and review because data work cannot tolerate black boxes.
